title: duSraBheja — Personal AI Second Brain & MCP Server
tagline: A personal open-brain that ingests everything I capture and makes it answerable by AI agents through MCP.
summary: The problem isn't capturing information — it's retrieving it when you need it and knowing what you know. duSraBheja runs on a single droplet, captures from Discord and six+ local sources, classifies and embeds everything, and exposes the result to Claude Code, Codex, and a public chat through MCP and HTTP.
resume_bullets: Built a Discord-fed personal AI second brain with an MCP server, a 6-layer canonical memory model (Evidence → Observation → Episode → Thread → Entity → Synthesis), and a 5-page private dashboard.
Pivoted from a TypeScript/WhatsApp/LangGraph stack to Python/Discord/ARQ on day 10; cut Anthropic + OpenAI months later for NVIDIA NIM free-tier models.
Hardened the public surface with an explicit fact allowlist, daily refresh, output scrubbing for repo URLs / IPs / SSNs / IPv6, and topic gating so the public chat only answers what I've approved.
demonstrates: Async Python at production scale (FastAPI + ARQ + asyncpg + pgvector).
Agentic orchestration with discipline — retrieval tests, confidence-gated routing, owner-approved publish path.
Self-hosted ops on a single droplet — five services, Docker Compose, no platform lock-in.
constraints: Free-tier model budget — every chat, vision, and embedding call routes through NIM; token counts and model names get logged but cost is zero.
Single-droplet ops — no horizontal scale, so the public path was engineered to be cache-friendly and fast.
outcomes: Live in production on a single DigitalOcean droplet running five services (brain-redis, brain-bot, brain-worker, brain-mcp, brain-api).
Powers the public chat at /brain and the Claude Code / Codex tool surface I use daily.
Captures from six+ sources, classifies with Llama 3.1 8B, embeds via nv-embedqa-e5-v5 (1024d), merges via Llama 3.3 70B.

curated_case_study.project_framing: A personal AI second brain that doesn't ask me to organize anything. I drop things into a Discord channel and ask later. The system handles the capture, classification, embedding, and merge into a canonical memory — and exposes the answer surface to Claude Code, Codex, and a public chat through MCP.
curated_case_study.problem: The problem isn't capturing information — it's retrieving it when you need it and knowing what you know. Notes app, browser bookmarks, Apple Notes, Discord screenshots, git history — knowledge lives in eight places and I never find it again. Every tool I tried required manual organization I don't actually do.
curated_case_study.why_now: MCP changed the answer surface. Once Claude Code and Codex could call into a brain over MCP, the second-brain problem stopped being about UI and started being about pipelines, retrieval quality, and what the public surface should and should not say.
curated_case_study.architecture_narrative: Discord #inbox is the capture entry point. The bot enqueues into ARQ on Redis; the worker runs an async pipeline — extract (PDF/image/excel/docx/link), classify (Llama 3.1 8B → strict JSON), embed (nv-embedqa-e5-v5, 1024d), librarian merge (Llama 3.3 70B) into a canonical Note. Classification is gated at 0.75 confidence — below that, a review queue and clarification flow. Storage is a six-layer canonical memory (Evidence → Observation → Episode → Thread → Entity → Synthesis); story is presentation, not storage. FastMCP exposes search / ask / capture / context / protocol / story tools to Claude Code and Codex. A 5-page private Atlas dashboard sits on the same data — every page is one or two indexed SQL queries, no on-render LLM calls. The public surface is a separate, owner-approved snapshot — PublicFactRecord rows that only surface when I check the approval queue.

product_flow.title: From capture to answer
product_flow.caption: How a single Discord message becomes something the brain can answer with.

steps.title: Capture
steps.detail: A message, image, PDF, or link lands in Discord #inbox. The bot enqueues a job, never blocks.

steps.title: Extract
steps.detail: Worker routes by MIME type and pulls text from PDFs, images (vision OCR), Excel, DOCX, or web links.

steps.title: Classify
steps.detail: Llama 3.1 8B returns strict JSON: category, confidence, entities, tags, suggested action. Below 0.75 → review queue.

steps.title: Embed
steps.detail: Text chunked at 512 tokens with 64 overlap, embedded via nv-embedqa-e5-v5 into 1024d vectors.

steps.title: Librarian merge
steps.detail: Llama 3.3 70B decides: merge into an existing Note or create a new canonical one. Source artifacts are kept with provenance.

steps.title: Answer
steps.detail: Same canonical store powers /brain chat, MCP tools for Claude Code & Codex, and the private dashboard.

branches.title: Low-confidence path
branches.detail: Items below the 0.75 gate get a clarification question and a review-queue row instead of being silently dropped.

branches.title: Cognition trigger
branches.detail: Every 20 merges, an on-demand synthesis pass runs across recent signals to surface threads I haven't seen.

product_flow.outcome: One brain, six capture surfaces, two answer surfaces.

architecture_diagram.title: System shape
architecture_diagram.caption: Five Docker services on one droplet; one canonical data store; two distinct answer surfaces.

nodes.label: Discord #inbox
nodes.detail: Bot cog enqueues every message + attachment as an ARQ job.

nodes.label: Local collector
nodes.detail: Apple Notes, Chrome history, git, project files, life exports — periodic scans.

nodes.label: ARQ worker
nodes.detail: Five concurrent jobs max, 5-minute timeout, async throughout.

nodes.label: Extract → Classify → Embed → Merge
nodes.detail: Strict JSON contracts at every step; failures stage to a review queue, not the dead-letter.

nodes.label: PostgreSQL + pgvector
nodes.detail: Canonical Notes + the six-layer memory (Evidence → Synthesis); vector search is project-aware.

nodes.label: AuditLog
nodes.detail: Every LLM call logs model, tokens, cost, duration, trace_id.

nodes.label: FastMCP server
nodes.detail: search / ask / capture / context / protocol / story tools for Claude Code & Codex.

nodes.label: Private dashboard
nodes.detail: Five Atlas pages — What's New, Inbox, Library, Projects, Public Facts. No on-render LLM calls.

nodes.label: Public surface
nodes.detail: Owner-approved PublicFactRecord allowlist + scrubbed chat. The site you're reading.

callouts.label: Confidence gate
callouts.body: 0.75 separates auto-accept from owner-review. Below the line, the brain asks a clarification question instead of guessing.

callouts.label: Public ≠ Private
callouts.body: The public site reads from a separate snapshot. Promoting anything to public is a manual approval — never an automatic derivation.

key_decisions.title: Pivot off TypeScript on day 10
key_decisions.decision: Rewrite the whole stack in Python with Discord/ARQ.
key_decisions.rationale: The TS stack had clean infra (NATS, Temporal, pgvector) but was bleeding memory across ten child processes and Node's async story wasn't native. Python + ARQ gave me native async/await, SQLAlchemy 2.0, real MCP, and simpler ops.
key_decisions.tradeoff: Threw away ten days of work. Bought a stack I could ship to production solo.

key_decisions.title: Drop Anthropic + OpenAI for NIM
key_decisions.decision: Move every chat, vision, and embedding call to NVIDIA NIM's free tier via one OpenAI-compatible client.
key_decisions.rationale: The cost curve was wrong for a personal brain. NIM free-tier has no per-token cost and the Llama 3.3 70B + nv-embedqa-e5-v5 quality is good enough for my retrieval and merge workloads.
key_decisions.tradeoff: Locked into NIM's free-tier rate limits and model lineup. Worth it for $0/month.

key_decisions.title: Owner-approved public surface, not derived
key_decisions.decision: Build a separate snapshot for the public site that only surfaces facts I've explicitly approved.
key_decisions.rationale: A second brain that auto-publishes is a liability. I want the brain to remember everything privately and only say in public what I've signed off on.
key_decisions.tradeoff: More owner work (approval queue) for a fundamentally safer public surface.

struggles.problem: Vector search silently returned wrong results for a day. asyncpg's parameter binding for pgvector isn't the same as psycopg2's — queries were running but ranking was nonsense.
struggles.resolution: Three-commit fix, about 65 lines. The deeper cost was confidence in retrieval. Recovery was discipline: every retrieval path got a test before being shipped, and the librarian and retriever got project-aware ranking. That habit is how I now ship retrieval changes without anxiety.

struggles.problem: Public snapshot rebuild silently wiped owner-curated content on every cron tick. A previous refactor stubbed the narrative builder and the rebuild kept calling it; months of approved content evaporated between deploys.
struggles.resolution: Two layers: a carry-forward in the rebuild so stub-derived fields preserve the previous snapshot's values, and an allowlist-gated project rebuild so the delete-then-write loop never deletes rows it can't replace. Then moved the narrative source out of code and into seed files in the repo.

learnings: Schema discipline survives rewrites; framework choices don't.
Retrieval tests are the cheapest insurance you can buy.
Anything that has to survive a refactor lives in a file or env var, not a Python function.
